summary: In the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus, Wittgenstein argues that the world is composed of facts rather than things, and that language relates to reality by acting as a logical picture of these facts. He asserts that the limits of language define the limits of thought, concluding that philosophy should be an activity of clarifying propositions rather than a theory, and that we must remain silent about what cannot be said clearly.

principles.statement: The world is the totality of facts, not of things.
principles.rationale: The world is determined by facts and their combinations in logical space. Things (objects) only have meaning in the context of their possible connections within atomic facts.
principles.source_quote: The world is the totality of facts, not of things.

principles.statement: What can be said at all can be said clearly; and whereof one cannot speak thereof one must be silent.
principles.rationale: Language has strict logical limits. Attempting to express thoughts that lie beyond the logical structure of language results in nonsense.
principles.source_quote: What can be said at all can be said clearly; and whereof one cannot speak thereof one must be silent.

principles.statement: Philosophy is an activity of clarification, not a body of doctrine.
principles.rationale: The goal of philosophy is not to produce 'philosophical propositions' but to logically clarify thoughts that are otherwise opaque and blurred.
principles.source_quote: Philosophy is not a theory but an activity. A philosophical work consists essentially of elucidations.

principles.statement: A proposition is a picture of reality.
principles.rationale: Propositions represent states of affairs by sharing a logical form with reality, much like a model or a physical picture.
principles.source_quote: The proposition is a picture of reality. The proposition is a model of the reality as we think it is.

frameworks.name: Logical Clarification of Thoughts
frameworks.when_to_apply: When encountering philosophical problems, deep confusions, or opaque statements.
steps: Recognize that most philosophical questions arise from misunderstanding the logic of language.
Translate the expressions into a logical symbolism that obeys logical grammar to avoid confusing different symbols.
Identify what can be clearly said (propositions of natural science) versus what can only be shown.
State the senselessness of questions that attempt to speak about the unspeakable.
frameworks.source_quote: The object of philosophy is the logical clarification of thoughts.

mental_models.name: The Picture Theory of Language
mental_models.description: Language maps to reality the way a picture maps to the scene it depicts. A picture models reality because its elements are arranged in a specific structure that corresponds to the arrangement of objects in reality. They share a 'logical form'.
mental_models.example: A musical score, a gramophone record, and sound waves all share a logical structure, allowing a musician to read the symphony from the score or reconstruct it from the record.

mental_models.name: Logical Space
mental_models.description: A conceptual framework where all possible states of affairs (atomic facts) exist. Objects and propositions exist within this space, and a proposition determines a specific 'logical place' within it.
mental_models.example: A speck in a visual field must have a color; it has a 'color space' around it. A tone must have a pitch.

heuristics: If a sign is not necessary then it is meaningless (Occam's razor).
What can be shown cannot be said.
Everything that can be thought at all can be thought clearly.

signature_quotes.text: The world is everything that is the case.
signature_quotes.context: The opening proposition of the Tractatus.

signature_quotes.text: The world is the totality of facts, not of things.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the fundamental composition of reality.

signature_quotes.text: What can be said at all can be said clearly; and whereof one cannot speak thereof one must be silent.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the whole meaning of the book in the preface.

signature_quotes.text: Philosophy is not a theory but an activity.
signature_quotes.context: Defining the proper role and nature of philosophical work.

signature_quotes.text: What can be shown cannot be said.
signature_quotes.context: Distinguishing between what language can state and what it merely exhibits.

signature_quotes.text: Most propositions and questions, that have been written about philosophical matters, are not false, but senseless.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the history of philosophical discourse.

anti_patterns.description: Attempting to say what can only be shown (e.g., trying to represent logical form in a proposition).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Propositions cannot represent logical form because logical form mirrors itself in the propositions. To represent it, we would have to step outside of logic and the world, which is impossible.

anti_patterns.description: Treating philosophy as a natural science or a body of doctrine.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Philosophy stands above or below the natural sciences. Its purpose is not to produce true propositions about the world, but to clarify the logical structure of thoughts.

anti_patterns.description: Using the same word to signify in different ways in everyday language.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates fundamental confusions. For example, using 'is' as a copula, a sign of equality, and an expression of existence leads to senseless pseudo-propositions.

anti_patterns.description: Asking questions about the existence of a formal concept (e.g., 'Are there objects?').
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Formal concepts are shown by the symbol itself, not expressed by propositions. No proposition can answer such a question, making it senseless.
